findings.title: API key stored in localStorage and sent as HTTP header
findings.description: The API key is persisted in localStorage via Zustand's persist middleware (storage key 'ai-cofounder-app-storage'). Any XSS vulnerability on the domain can exfiltrate it. The key is then sent as an 'x-api-key' header on every request, exposing it to network inspection and intermediate proxies.
findings.fix: Implemented server-side API key cache. Key is now sent to POST /api/auth/api-key once, stored in server memory keyed by JWT userId, and never touches localStorage. The requireApiKey middleware checks the in-memory cache before falling back to the header.

findings.title: Prompt injection defenses use a blacklist approach
findings.description: sanitizeUserInput relies on regex-based pattern matching for known jailbreak phrases (PROMPT_INJECTION_PATTERNS). Blacklist-based filtering is inherently incomplete — novel injection techniques, encoded payloads, or subtle rewordings can bypass detection. The system boundary suffix is a good defense-in-depth layer but is appended as plaintext and could be included in training data.
findings.recommendation: Add an allowlist-based input classifier, integrate a dedicated prompt-injection detection model (e.g., shieldgemma), or use structured output formatting (JSON mode) for all AI calls. Also consider querying the AI to classify whether the input is adversarial before processing.

findings.title: extractJSON can throw generic errors from AI output
findings.description: When the AI returns malformed JSON that does not contain braces or brackets, extractJSON throws an Error with the raw AI output snippet (first 200 chars) in the message. This error propagates through sendError and may log potentially sensitive AI output in the server logs.
findings.recommendation: Truncate or sanitize the error message before throwing, or use a more robust JSON extraction that gracefully handles failure.

findings.title: sanitizeOutput removes script tags but not all XSS vectors
findings.description: sanitizeOutput strips <script> tags and file:// URIs but does not remove event handlers (onclick=, onerror=), javascript: URIs, or other HTML injection vectors in the AI output that could be rendered unsafely by the frontend.
findings.recommendation: Use a dedicated HTML sanitization library (DOMPurify) on the frontend before rendering AI output, or extend sanitizeOutput to strip all HTML tags.

findings.title: Custom error status codes could leak internal information
findings.description: sanitizeError checks if (error.status && Number.isInteger(error.status)) and passes through the status and raw message in development mode. In production (isProd), it falls back to the generic message, but the status code is still used as-is. If an internal module sets a custom status, it could confuse API clients.
findings.recommendation: Validate custom status codes against a whitelist of allowed HTTP status codes (e.g., 400-599) and ensure they are integers.

findings.title: Password policy only requires minimum length
findings.description: Password validation only checks length >= 8 characters. There is no requirement for complexity (uppercase, lowercase, digits, special characters), no breached-password check, and no account lockout mechanism after repeated failed login attempts.
findings.recommendation: Enforce a stronger password policy (e.g., zxcvbn-based strength check), implement exponential backoff or account lockout after 5 failed attempts, and consider rate-limiting per-email in addition to per-IP.

findings.title: JWT includes email in the payload
findings.description: The JWT payload contains both userId and email. While not a direct vulnerability, including personally identifiable information (PII) in tokens increases the impact if a token is leaked — the email is recoverable by base64-decoding the payload.
findings.recommendation: Minimize JWT payload to only userId. The email can be fetched from /api/auth/me when needed.

findings.title: Global rate limit may affect legitimate users
findings.description: The global rate limiter is set to 100 requests per 15 minutes (about 6.6 req/min). This is aggressive for a dashboard application that may make many API calls during normal use (chat streaming, research, memory operations). The auth rate limiter at 10 requests/15min could block legitimate password reset flows.
findings.recommendation: Increase the global limit to 300-500 requests per 15 minutes, or apply per-endpoint rate limits more granularly. Add a retry-after header in the rate limit response.

findings.title: Supabase service_role key used with full table access
findings.description: The application uses SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY (service_role key) which bypasses all Row-Level Security (RLS) policies. If this key is compromised, an attacker has full read/write access to the entire database. The key is also loaded from process.env at runtime and could leak via error messages or debug logging.
findings.recommendation: Restrict the service key to a secrets manager or environment-specific access. Enable RLS on all Supabase tables and use the anon key with restricted JWT claims for client-side operations. Ensure the service key is never logged or exposed in error responses.

findings.title: Missing CASCADE deletes on user foreign keys
findings.description: The users table has no CASCADE deletes configured. If a user record is deleted, orphaned rows remain in founders, businesses, tasks, memory_nodes, and memory_edges tables.
findings.recommendation: Add ON DELETE CASCADE to foreign key constraints referencing users(id), or implement a cleanup function when users are deactivated.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET example value is insecure
findings.description: The example value 'your-secret-key-change-in-production' is clearly a placeholder, which is correct for a template. However, there is no documentation about the required entropy (minimum 256 bits) or a command to generate one.
findings.recommendation: Add a comment with a key generation command: 'Generate with: openssl rand -hex 64' and document the minimum length requirement.

findings.title: Missing ALLOW_SERVER_KEY_FALLBACK documentation
findings.description: The ALLOW_SERVER_KEY_FALLBACK environment variable controls whether the server's NVIDIA_API_KEY can be used by unauthenticated users. This has significant security implications (unlimited AI usage billing) but is not documented in .env.example.
findings.recommendation: Add ALLOW_SERVER_KEY_FALLBACK=false to .env.example with a warning about the billing implications of enabling it.

findings.title: CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' for styles
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy includes style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline', which allows inline style injection. While react apps commonly need this for CSS-in-JS, it reduces CSP effectiveness against CSS-based data exfiltration attacks.
findings.recommendation: Consider using a nonce-based CSP for styles if the frontend framework supports it, or evaluate whether inline styles can be replaced with class-based styling.

findings.title: No rate limiting at reverse proxy level
findings.description: All rate limiting is handled at the application level. If the Node.js process is overwhelmed, the nginx layer has no protection against request flooding.
findings.recommendation: Add nginx-level rate limiting using limit_req_zone and limit_req directives to provide defense-in-depth before requests reach the application.

findings.title: User message is directly interpolated into AI prompt with limited sanitization
findings.recommendation: Apply sanitizeUserInput to ALL user-controlled fields before prompt construction, including context values. Consider a strict schema validation for context objects.

findings.title: No CSRF tokens on state-changing endpoints
findings.description: POST endpoints do not validate CSRF tokens or use SameSite cookies. While most endpoints require JWT or API key headers (which browsers do not automatically send cross-origin), the /api/reminders/register endpoint only validates body params without authentication, making it theoretically vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.
findings.recommendation: Add SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax cookie attributes if session cookies are introduced. For endpoints without auth headers, validate Origin and Referer headers.

findings.title: WhatsApp reminder registration is unauthenticated
findings.description: The POST /api/reminders/register endpoint accepts email and phone without any authentication. While validated for format, the data is stored in an in-memory registry without user context, and the endpoint is globally rate-limited but could be used to spam the reminder system.
findings.recommendation: Require JWT authentication for the reminders endpoint or tie the phone number to the authenticated user's email.

key_recommendations: CRITICAL: API key in localStorage — FIXED via server-side cache
HIGH: Supabase service_role key usage — restrict to a secrets manager
MEDIUM: Prompt injection — move from blacklist to allowlist/ML-based detection
MEDIUM: Password policy — add complexity requirements and lockout
MEDIUM: Rate limits — adjust to avoid blocking legitimate users
MEDIUM: User input sanitization — apply to context objects as well
